ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific 6-count portion code; A0CKP (fried onion rings) used as the closest parent for the battered deep-fried preparation. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific portion-defined product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for onion rings identified in the clinical terminology browser; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of 6 onion rings weighs approximately 85 g, equivalent to a typical fast-food side-order portion.

nutrient_source.title: Onion Rings, Battered, Fried — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at 0.95 confidence for all per-100g nutrient fields. Values are preserved as supplied. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Onion rings, breaded, par fried, frozen, prepared, heated in oven' (FDC ID 170697) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; the USDA value of 4.8 g/100g saturated fat is used as a supplementary estimate and documented accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for battered deep-fried onion rings exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 75 is estimated by analogy to 'white wheat flour-based battered fried foods' (GI ~72–78) as documented in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database entries for similar battered fried starchy foods. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (75) × available carbohydrate per serving (41.0 g/100g × 85 g serving / 100) / 100 = 75 × 34.85 / 100 = 26.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 780 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid onion rings; a single 85 g serving delivers approximately 663 mg sodium, representing more than 40% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg, and the deep-fried saturated fat content further contributes to arterial stiffness and elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~75) combined with 41 g/100g refined carbohydrate and only 2.4 g/100g dietary fibre produces a high glycemic load of 26 per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit onion rings because the high-GI battered coating causes rapid postprandial glucose elevation, the glycemic load of 26 per standard serving exceeds the high-GL threshold of 20, and the fat-carbohydrate combination further delays but prolongs glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (22.2 g/100g) with estimated saturated fat 4.8 g/100g, very high sodium 780 mg/100g, and deep-frying process generating potentially atherogenic oxidised lipids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid onion rings; the combination of high saturated fat, very high sodium, and oxidised fats from deep-frying collectively raises LDL cholesterol, promotes endothelial inflammation, and elevates blood pressure, all of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and oxidised lipids that amplify pro-inflammatory cytokine activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried battered foods such as onion rings because AGEs formed during high-temperature frying directly stimulate RAGE receptors and TNF-α pathways, worsening synovial inflammation and joint damage.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion Rings (6 Count) are a Western deep-fried snack consisting of thick-cut onion slices coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and submerged in hot oil until the exterior is golden-brown and crisp while the interior onion softens. The texture contrast between the crunchy batter shell and the tender, slightly sweet onion interior is the defining sensory characteristic. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly sweet from the onion, and rich from the frying oil, with a pronounced saltiness (780 mg sodium per 100 g). Nutritionally, onion rings are energy-dense at 385 kcal/100g, with a high carbohydrate load of 41 g/100g from the refined batter, moderate protein at 5.29 g/100g, and high fat at 22.2 g/100g. Dietary fibre is low at 2.4 g/100g. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 75 (high), producing a glycemic load of approximately 26 per standard 6-ring serving of 85 g. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension (extreme sodium load), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI and GL), or cardiovascular disease (high saturated fat, sodium, and oxidised lipids from frying). In Singapore, onion rings are commonly served as a fast-food side dish or snack at Western quick-service restaurants. The HPB Singapore Foodo database is the primary nutrient source for this record.
common_user_queries: How many calories in 6 onion rings?
Is it okay to eat onion rings with high blood pressure?
Are onion rings suitable for heart patients?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at 0.95 confidence as stated in the input record provenance. Saturated fat is supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170697) as an approved secondary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, which slightly reduces overall confidence for the GI component but not for the nutrient profile.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (4.8 g/100g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Onion rings, breaded, par fried, frozen, prepared, heated in oven' (FDC ID 170697) because HPB Foodo does not separately report saturated fat for this item. This is an approved secondary source substitution. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific HPB-sourced onion ring preparation are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of April 2026. 3. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried wheat-flour foods in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this specific product exists. 4. This food is classified as Western cuisine; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null as onion rings are not a Singaporean-origin dish requiring mandatory multilingual coverage under G6. 5. The input record listed 'rheumatoid_arthritis' as a health suitability condition; this has been preserved in additional_conditions with a clinical note. 6. Kosher status is marked false as deep-frying in shared oil with non-kosher items is common in commercial settings; this should be verified per specific vendor. 7. FoodEx2 match type corrected to 'closest_parent' (from 'analogous_estimate' used in prior iteration) as 'closest_parent' is the correct allowed value from the set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] for a case where the code represents the nearest parent category rather than an exact match.
